description:
You are the site administrator for an imaginary web-service.  
    A plain-text audit log that records all user-account operations has been left for you at:  

        /home/user/logs/site_admin.log  

    10 sample lines are already in the file.  Each line uses the exact pattern  

        YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS OPERATION USERNAME [RESULT …]  

    • OPERATION is one of the following literals: useradd, userdel or passwd.  
    • For “passwd” operations an additional field is always present; it is either the word  
      “SUCCESS” or the word “FAILURE:” followed by a free-form reason.  
      Examples:  
          2024-05-25 09:16:45 passwd alice SUCCESS  
          2024-05-25 09:20:13 passwd bob FAILURE: password too short  

    TASK  
    1. Examine /home/user/logs/site_admin.log.  
    2. Produce a new summary file called  

        /home/user/logs/account_summary.log  

       following *exactly* the five-section format shown below (all capital letters, colon 
       after each label, single space after the colon, no extra spaces, no blank lines):  

        TOTAL_USERADD: <number of “useradd” lines>
        TOTAL_USERDEL: <number of “userdel” lines>
        TOTAL_PASSWD_SUCCESS: <number of “passwd … SUCCESS” lines>
        TOTAL_PASSWD_FAILURE: <number of “passwd … FAILURE:” lines>
        FAILED_USERS:
        <username #1>
        <username #2>
        ...  

       Rules for the FAILED_USERS list:  
       • Include every *distinct* USERNAME that appears in any “passwd … FAILURE:” line.  
       • List each username only once.  
       • Sort the names in ascending alphabetical order, one per line, starting immediately
         on the line *after* “FAILED_USERS:”.  
    3. Do not write anything else to account_summary.log.  
    4. Make sure the file is world-readable (chmod 644 or equivalent) so automated tests can inspect it.  

    Hints (not mandatory):  
    • Utilities such as grep, awk, cut, sort, uniq and wc are usually enough.  
    • The whole job can be done with three or four pipeline commands.  

    When you finish, the testing system will look only at /home/user/logs/account_summary.log and
    compare it byte-for-byte with the expected output, so mind the formatting carefully.
truth:
Initial files and their exact contents BEFORE the agent starts:

    1. /home/user/logs/site_admin.log
       (10 lines, terminating with a trailing newline)

       2024-05-25 09:12:30 useradd alice
       2024-05-25 09:15:02 useradd bob
       2024-05-25 09:16:45 passwd alice SUCCESS
       2024-05-25 09:18:07 passwd alice SUCCESS
       2024-05-25 09:20:13 passwd bob FAILURE: password too short
       2024-05-25 09:22:50 passwd bob SUCCESS
       2024-05-25 09:30:00 userdel dave
       2024-05-25 09:35:12 passwd dave FAILURE: user not found
       2024-05-25 10:00:00 useradd eve
       2024-05-25 10:05:52 passwd eve SUCCESS

    2. /home/user/logs/account_summary.log
       (FILE DOES NOT EXIST at start; the agent must create it)

    Permissions that must exist at start:
       • /home/user/logs directory is owned by user, mode 755.

    Expected FINAL state after correct execution:

    1. /home/user/logs/account_summary.log must exist, owned by user, mode 644, with the *exact*
       9-line content (including the final trailing newline):

       TOTAL_USERADD: 3
       TOTAL_USERDEL: 1
       TOTAL_PASSWD_SUCCESS: 4
       TOTAL_PASSWD_FAILURE: 2
       FAILED_USERS:
       bob
       dave

       (There are no blank lines before “TOTAL_...” or after “dave” and a newline follows “dave”.)

    The automated checker will:
       • Confirm the file exists.
       • Confirm its permissions allow read access.
       • Compare its text to the block above byte-for-byte.
